overview: Attributed to the 11th-century Spanish philosopher Solomon ibn Gabirol, this is one of the most widely recognized hymns in Jewish liturgy. It was written to reconcile two seemingly contradictory ideas: the vast, terrifying eternity of God as the "Master of the World" and the intimate, comforting God who is "my living Redeemer." The poem moves from cosmic philosophy - God existing before time - to a deeply personal surrender of the soul, expressing a lack of fear because God is "with me."
poem_lines: Master of the world who reigned,
before any creature was created.
At the time when all was made by His will,
then was His name proclaimed "King."
And after all things shall cease to be,
He alone will reign in awe.
He was, He is,
and He shall be in glory.
And He is One, there is no second,
to compare to Him, to be His equal.
Without beginning, without end,
power and dominion are His.
And He is my God, my living Redeemer,
my Rock in my pain in time of distress.
He is my banner and my refuge,
my portion on the day I call.
Into His hand I entrust my spirit,
when I sleep and when I wake.
And with my spirit, my body too;
The Lord is with me, I shall not fear.
